next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 17

The ready room was underground. Lena could feel the venue above her in the weight of the concrete, the closed-in cooled air, the forty thousand seats waiting over her head. She sat with her towel across her knees and her goggles in one hand, and the quiet in her skull, familiar now, made everything else sound overdescribed.

Seven other swimmers waited with her. She knew most of them by shoulders, kick timing, and the habits that survived from meet to meet. Adelaïde Moreau stretched in the corner with the saint medallion she always tucked into her suit before the walk out. Kaede Watanabe had her eyes closed and her lips moving through some private rehearsal. Nina Okoro paced the far wall with three fingers taped bright green. The room held that smooth patched readiness Lena could read before anyone touched the water.

"Morozova. Lane five."

The corridor to the pool deck was fluorescent and cold. By the time she stepped into the open, the lights had already found her. They came down hard enough to glaze the water and bleach the color out of people's faces. The noise of the crowd hit her in the sternum before it made sense in her ears. Lane five waited in the middle of a rectangle of blue-green water that looked almost fixed in place under the broadcast rig.

She climbed onto the block. Moreau stood in lane four. Watanabe was in three. Lena bent, settled her feet, and let the small useless wish pass through her that the dive would arrive already solved. Then the tone sounded and she went.

The water hit her all at once, blunt and grainy instead of opening into...
open_hooks_to_carry: Compressed time: most athletes signed Meridian's deal; Kai Lena and a few others run the partial patch or nothing
The meal: Yusuf's biryani, real conversation, laughter that is brief and genuine
Lena describes the silence in her head—learning to think without the system's ambient input
prev_tail_excerpt:
...d up sitting on the edge of the dock. Cold concrete pressed through his pants. Cold metal stayed in his wrist. Fine. He could feel both.

"And the virus," he said. "You knew."

"I figured it out in February. The bidirectional pathway. Every time I connected, there was a propagation risk." She looked at the bare wrist. "I kept connecting. Seventy-seven sessions after that."

She met his eyes with the stripped version of herself and nothing around it. She offered no defense and no performance of remorse.

"I knew what I was doing," she said. "I could've stopped. I kept going because stopping meant being just me, in my own body, with my own senses, and that wasn't enough." She looked at the bare wrist again. "There's no deeper version than that."

He saw Lena at the pool wall with water running off her shoulders, asking what had happened to the last two laps. Margot had called it acceptable.

"I'm sorry," Margot said. The words were plain and asked for nothing. "About Lena. About what I did to her."

Kai stood. The backs of his thighs were cold. His wrist throbbed with the pressure change. The mountains stayed where they were, patient and indifferent.

"Yeah," he said.

He went back inside. Behind him, Margot stayed on the crate in the cold. He didn't look back to see whether she was still sitting there or whether Tomas's phantom had taken her leg again. The loading-dock door shut behind him, and the warmth on his face felt like re-entering a building where everything was wrong and would stay wrong and the opening ceremony was five weeks away and Tomas's door was still closed.
state_deltas_required: Time has passed and choices are made; what remains is the human baseline beneath the crisis
A moment of genuine human quiet before the public reckoning; the relationships are damaged but real
